capDescription: 8-20 cm wide, bright crimson red fading to orange-red, covered in white to cream pyramidal warts (volva remnants). Sticky when wet.
gillDescription: White, free from the stem, crowded, broad. Short gills present.
stemDescription: 10-20 cm long, white, solid, base bulbous with concentric rings of cottony scales. Features a soft white hanging skirt-like ring.
lookAlikes: Amanita xanthocephala (Yellow-orange flygills, smaller, yellow cap parts)
notes: Introduced species, highly toxic/hallucinogenic. Forms spectacular mycorrhizal rings around Pinus radiata in Victorian plantations.

capDescription: 2-5 cm wide, conical to convex, hygrophanous. Caramel brown when moist, drying to pale straw yellow. Margin often translucent and slightly striated when wet.
gillDescription: Adnate to adnexed, close, pale brown turning dark purplish-brown as spores mature. Edges remaining paler.
stemDescription: 5-12 cm long, white to dingy silver-grey, fibrous, tough. Base often covered in dense white mycelial cords.
bruisingReaction: Intense blue-green bruising on all parts when handled or damaged.
lookAlikes: Galerina patagonica (Yellow-brown spore print, no blue bruising, central ring zone)
Hypholoma fasciculare (Sulfur tuft, bright yellow-green gills, bitter taste, no blue bruising)
notes: Native to SE Australia. Potently psychoactive (contains psilocybin). Can grow in abundance in municipal mulch gardens in Melbourne and Victoria hills.

capDescription: 7-20 cm wide, funnel-shaped (infundibuliform) or fan-shaped. Creamy white with shades of purple, dark grey, or bronze near the center.
gillDescription: White to cream, deeply decurrent (running down the stem), crowded. Bioluminescent (glows soft green in complete darkness).
stemDescription: 2-8 cm long, lateral or off-center, thick, firm, matching cap color.
bruisingReaction: No bruising reaction, but gills emit a dim green light.
lookAlikes: Pleurotus ostreatus (Oyster Mushroom - edible, not native, gills not bioluminescent, typically uniform color)
notes: Highly toxic, causing severe vomiting. Celebrated for its remarkable bioluminescence due to luciferin-luciferase reaction, visible to dark-adapted eyes.

capDescription: 5-20 cm wide, convex then flat-convex, bright orange to tawny-yellow, dry, finely covered in tiny fibrillose scales. Fleshy and robust.
gillDescription: Adnate to decurrent with a tooth, yellow-orange turning rusty orange as spores ripen.
stemDescription: 8-15 cm long, thick, swollen in center or base, solid, yellowish-orange. Features a high, robust, membranous persistent rings.
lookAlikes: Armillaria luteobubalina (Australian Honey Fungus - white spore print, lacks intense orange color)
notes: Bitter taste. Often found in massive, visually stunning clusters at the base of Eucalyptus trees across Victoria.

capDescription: 0.5-2 cm wide, fan-shaped to kidney-shaped, bright orange to reddish-orange. Distinctly honeycomb-like or pore-like beneath, visible from above structurally.
gillDescription: Replaced by large, conspicuous hexagonal pores (0.5-1 mm wide), glowing brilliant orange.
stemDescription: 0.2-1.5 cm long, lateral, slender, bright orange, attaching Cap offset to wood.
lookAlikes: Cruentomycena viscidocruenta (Ruby Bonnet, tiny red cap, normal gills, not pores)
notes: Invasive species native to Madagascar. Rapidly spreading through wet gullies of Victoria (e.g. Otway Ranges, Dandenongs), displacing native saprotrophs.
